planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before fall transplant; in zone 9 transplant September–October for a November–February harvest. Cardinale is a mid-season radicchio that heads reliably in zone 9 fall conditions without requiring excessively cold temperatures to form tight heads. The color deepens and bitterness mellows after exposure to several light frosts.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Cardinale is an Italian radicchio variety producing medium-sized, round to slightly elongated heads with deep garnet-red to cardinal-red leaves and white midribs — the color is consistently deeper and more saturated than many other radicchio varieties, which gives it its name. The bitter-sweet, complex radicchio flavor is characteristic of the Cichorium intybus species, with the bitterness mellowing appropriately after cold exposure. Cardinale is valued by market growers for its reliable head formation in variable fall weather and its consistent deep color that holds through the harvest and storage period. In Italian cooking, Cardinale works in all standard radicchio preparations — grilled, braised, in risotto, in pasta, or raw in salads where its deep color provides dramatic visual contrast. In zone 9 the fall-winter-early spring season produces the most deeply colored and best-flavored heads, with improvement after the first frosts.
sources: Seeds of Italy — Cardinale Radicchio Growing Notes
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Radicchio Varieties
University of California Cooperative Extension — Radicchio Production Guide

planting.note: Direct sow in fall; in zone 9 sow September–October for harvest November through February. Catalogna can also be sown in spring (January–February) for a shorter harvest before summer heat causes bolting. The dandelion-like leaves are most tender and mild when harvested young at 60–70 days; older plants become progressively more bitter. Harvest the whole rosette or strip outer leaves for continuous production.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Catalogna Chicory (Cicoria di Catalogna, also called Asparagus Chicory or Puntarelle in its bolted form) is the traditional Italian cut-leaf chicory of Lazio and Apulia, producing elongated, deeply toothed, dandelion-shaped leaves with a pleasantly bitter, refreshing flavor that is considerably milder than radicchio. In Italy the outer leaves are eaten braised or in soups, while the inner pale stems are prized as the luxury green 'puntarelle' — the hollow, pale, crunchy stem shoots that emerge when the plant begins to bolt, traditionally served raw in Rome with an anchovy-garlic-lemon dressing. Catalogna is one of the most widely eaten cooking greens in southern Italy and is slowly gaining recognition in North American specialty markets. In zone 9 it provides a productive cut-and-come-again winter green harvest from fall sowings, and the mild temperatures allow it to slowly develop the inner stem shoots (puntarelle) that make it exceptional. Harvest outer leaves throughout winter and allow the center to develop stem shoots in late winter for puntarelle.
sources: Seeds of Italy — Catalogna Chicory Growing Notes
Slow Food Foundation — Puntarelle di Catalogna
University of California Cooperative Extension — Italian Chicory Varieties

planting.note: Start indoors 3–4 weeks before transplant; in zone 9 transplant September–October for fall harvest. Direct sowing is also possible. Frisee blanches naturally at the center as the outer leaves close over the interior; for the maximum pale center and milder flavor, tie the outer leaves loosely with twine 2 weeks before harvest. In zone 9 fall planting gives the most reliable head development; spring planting bolts too quickly in warming conditions.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Frisee is the fine-leaved, deeply curled, frizzy form of curly endive — a classic French bistro salad ingredient produced by its pale, blanched interior heart surrounded by a halo of fine, frizzy dark-green outer leaves. The pale interior (the blanched portion) has a mild, gently bitter, nutty flavor that is the defining taste of the classic frisee salad with lardons and a warm poached egg — one of the most recognizable French bistro dishes. The dark green outer leaves are more bitter and are usually removed or used for cooking. Frisee blanches naturally when the outer leaves overlap and shade the center, but deliberate blanching (tying the outer leaves for 1–2 weeks before harvest) produces a larger, more uniform pale center. In zone 9 frisee is a fall-through-winter crop that produces its characteristic delicate, pale center in the cool, short-day conditions from November through February.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Frisee Endive Variety Notes
Territorial Seed Company — Curly Endive and Frisee
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Endive and Chicory

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before fall transplant; in zone 9 transplant August–September for harvest November–January. Batavian endive takes longer to mature than frisee; allow the full 80–95 days from transplant. Blanch the center by tying outer leaves together for 10–14 days before harvest to reduce bitterness and lighten the interior. The large, robust heads store well for 2–3 weeks under refrigeration.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained, consistently moist soil in full sun; Broad-Leaf Batavian Endive is the flat-leafed, large-headed escarole-type endive — distinct from frizzy curly endive in having broad, wavy, slightly ruffled leaves rather than finely cut, frilly ones. The large, open heads weigh 1–2 pounds with pale green to cream-white interior leaves (if blanched) and a mild, slightly bitter flavor that is more approachable than radicchio or frisee. Broad-Leaf Batavian endive is extensively used in Italian cooking — sautéed with garlic and olive oil, incorporated into soups and braised bean dishes, and used as a pizza or pasta topping where its mild bitterness counterbalances richer ingredients. It is also used raw in salads when blanched to a creamy interior. The large, substantial leaves hold up better to cooking than frisee or standard lettuces. In zone 9 it is a reliable fall crop that matures through November and December for harvest into January.
sources: Seeds of Italy — Batavian Endive Growing Notes
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Endive Types
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Endive and Escarole Production

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before fall transplant; in zone 9 transplant August–September for harvest November–January. Full Heart Batavian self-blanches at the center more reliably than other escarole types as the outer leaves naturally close over the heart. In zone 9 the mild fall allows slow, steady head development from September transplants through November harvest.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into fertile, well-drained, consistently moist soil in full sun; Full Heart Batavian is the classic escarole for Italian home cooking — a broad-leaved chicory endive forming a large, dense head with a characteristic self-blanching, creamy-yellow interior heart surrounded by darker outer leaves. The center heart has the mildest, most tender flavor of any escarole or endive type; the outer leaves are more bitter and are often removed or reserved for long-cooked soups and braises. Full Heart Batavian is the standard escarole used in Italian-American cooking — the primary green in Italian wedding soup (minestra maritata), in braised escarole with white beans and garlic, and as a pizza topping. The dense, substantial head stores well for 2–3 weeks in the refrigerator after harvest, making escarole one of the most practical winter greens for home cooks. In zone 9 the fall growing season from August transplants produces excellent heads by November; the plants are cold-hardy and withstand light frosts that improve flavor.
sources: Seeds of Italy — Full Heart Batavian Escarole Growing Notes
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Escarole
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Escarole and Endive Production
